title: Time-to-Event Endpoints — Simulated Oncology RWE
text: This vignette demonstrates a reproducible RWE survival analysis pipeline applied to a simulated oncology RCT (n = 400) with overall survival (OS) as the primary endpoint. Methods progress from descriptive Kaplan–Meier estimation through multivariable Cox regression, inverse probability weighting (IPW) for the average treatment effect (ATE), and E-value sensitivity analysis for unmeasured confounding — mirroring regulatory RWE practice for oncology submissions.

title: Time-to-Event Endpoints — Simulated Oncology RWE
text:
1 Risk-Set Adjusted Kaplan–Meier


Show code
fit <- survfit(Surv(os_time, os_event) ~ trt, data = data_onc)
ggsurvfit(fit) +
  add_confidence_interval() +
  add_risktable() +
  add_pvalue(caption = "Log-rank {p.value}") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("0" = "#E34234", "1" = "#2E5F8A"),
                     labels = c("Control", "Treatment")) +
  labs(title = "Overall Survival by Treatment Arm",
       x = "Time (months)", y = "Survival Probability")





Figure 1. Kaplan–Meier Overall Survival by Treatment Arm.






Show code
survfit(Surv(os_time, os_event) ~ trt, data = data_onc) |>
  tbl_survfit(
    probs    = 0.5,
    label    = list(trt ~ "Treatment Arm"),
    label_header = "**Median OS (95% CI)**"
  ) |>
  modify_header(label ~ "**Treatment Arm**") |>
  bold_labels() |>
  as_gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Median Overall Survival by Treatment Arm") |>
  tab_footnote(footnote = "Kaplan–Meier estimate. CI: 95% confidence interval (Brookmeyer-Crowley method).") |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size           = px(13),
    heading.title.font.size   = px(14),
    heading.title.font.weight = "bold",
    table.width               = pct(100)
  )






Table 0. Median Overall Survival with 95% Confidence Intervals by Treatment Arm.






Median Overall Survival by Treatment Arm

Median OS (95% CI)

title: Time-to-Event Endpoints — Simulated Oncology RWE
text:
2 Cox Proportional Hazards Model


Show code
cox_os <- coxph(
  Surv(os_time, os_event) ~ trt + age + ecog + stage,
  data = data_onc, ties = "efron"
)

broom::tidy(cox_os, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(estimate, conf.low, conf.high), decimals = 2) |>
  tab_header(title = "Cox Proportional Hazards Model") |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(footnote = "Multivariable Cox model (trt + age + stage + ecog).") |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size           = px(13),
    heading.title.font.size   = px(14),
    heading.title.font.weight = "bold",
    table.width               = pct(100)
  )

Multivariable Cox model (trt + age + stage + ecog).










Show code
ph_test <- cox.zph(cox_os)
ggcoxzph(ph_test)





Figure 2. Schoenfeld residuals test for PH assumption.






Show code
ggforest(cox_os, data = data_onc,
         main     = "Forest Plot — OS",
         fontsize = 0.85)





Figure 3. Forest Plot — HRs with 95% CIs for all covariates.




The adjusted HR for treatment = 0.97 (95% CI: 0.77, 1.24). A HR < 1 favors treatment (reduced hazard of death); a HR > 1 favors control. The Schoenfeld residuals plot above assesses the proportional hazards assumption — non-random trends over time would indicate a PH violation requiring model extension (e.g., time-varying coefficients or stratification).

title: Time-to-Event Endpoints — Simulated Oncology RWE
section: 3 IPW / AIPW for ATE with Robust SE
text:
3 IPW / AIPW for ATE with Robust SE
Doubly robust estimation on oncology data.

WeightIt: propensity score model (trt ~ age + stage + ecog), estimand=“ATE”
IPW-weighted Cox model for ATE with sandwich SE (regulatory practice)
E-value for the primary IPW HR (unmeasured confounding sensitivity)



Show code
# IPTW model
w_out   <- weightit(trt ~ age + stage + ecog, data = data_onc,
                    method = "glm", estimand = "ATE")
cox_ipw <- coxph(Surv(os_time, os_event) ~ trt,
                 data = data_onc, weights = w_out$weights, robust = TRUE)

# Robust HR and CI
s    <- summary(cox_ipw)
beta <- coef(cox_ipw)["trt"]
se   <- s$coefficients["trt", "robust se"]
hr   <- exp(beta)
lo   <- exp(beta - qnorm(0.975) * se)
hi   <- exp(beta + qnorm(0.975) * se)

# E-values — evalues.HR() returns a matrix; extract numerically before building tibble
ev_mat <- evalues.HR(est = hr, lo = lo, hi = hi, rare = FALSE)
e_est  <- as.numeric(ev_mat[1, 1])
e_ci   <- as.numeric(ev_mat[1, 2])

results_tbl <- tibble(
  Covariate       = "trt",
  HR              = hr,
  Lower95CI       = lo,
  Upper95CI       = hi,
  EValue_Estimate = e_est,
  EValue_CI_Limit = e_ci
)

results_tbl |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(
    columns  = c(HR, Lower95CI, Upper95CI, EValue_Estimate, EValue_CI_Limit),
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Covariate       = "Treatment",
    HR              = "Hazard Ratio",
    Lower95CI       = "95% CI Lower",
    Upper95CI       = "95% CI Upper",
    EValue_Estimate = "E-value",
    EValue_CI_Limit = "E-value (CI Limit)"
  ) |>
  tab_header(title = "IPW-Weighted Cox Model with E-value Sensitivity Analysis") |>
  tab_footnote(footnote = "ATE IPTW model using age, stage, and ECOG score.")






Table 2. IPW-Weighted Cox Model with E-value Sensitivity Analysis.


IPW-Weighted Cox Model with E-value Sensitivity Analysis


Treatment
Hazard Ratio
95% CI Lower
95% CI Upper
E-value
E-value (CI Limit)




trt
0.95
0.75
1.21
0.97
0.82



ATE IPTW model using age, stage, and ECOG score.








IPW-weighted Cox model for ATE with sandwich SE resulted in a HR = 0.95 (95% CI: 0.75, 1.21). The E-value quantifies the minimum strength of association an unmeasured confounder would need with both treatment and outcome to explain away the observed effect — larger values indicate greater robustness to unmeasured confounding.

title: Time-to-Event Endpoints — Simulated Oncology RWE
section: 4 Doubly Robust AIPW for RMST Difference
text:
4 Doubly Robust AIPW for RMST Difference
Augmented IPW (AIPW) combines an outcome model with the propensity score to achieve double robustness — consistent even if one model is misspecified.


Show code
# Outcome models: E[RMST24 | X, A=1] and E[RMST24 | X, A=0]
data_onc$rmst24_tmp <- pmin(data_onc$os_time, 24)
mu1 <- predict(lm(rmst24_tmp ~ age + stage + ecog,
                  data = data_onc[data_onc$trt == 1, ]),
               newdata = data_onc)
mu0 <- predict(lm(rmst24_tmp ~ age + stage + ecog,
                  data = data_onc[data_onc$trt == 0, ]),
               newdata = data_onc)

# Propensity scores from WeightIt object
ps  <- w_out$ps
A   <- data_onc$trt
Y   <- data_onc$rmst24_tmp

# AIPW influence function
phi <- (A / ps) * (Y - mu1) + mu1 -
       ((1 - A) / (1 - ps)) * (Y - mu0) - mu0

aipw_est <- mean(phi)
aipw_se  <- sd(phi) / sqrt(length(phi))
aipw_lo  <- aipw_est - qnorm(0.975) * aipw_se
aipw_hi  <- aipw_est + qnorm(0.975) * aipw_se
aipw_z   <- aipw_est / aipw_se
aipw_p   <- 2 * pnorm(-abs(aipw_z))

tibble(
  Analysis  = "AIPW — RMST Difference at 24 Months",
  Estimate  = aipw_est,
  SE        = aipw_se,
  `95% CI Lower` = aipw_lo,
  `95% CI Upper` = aipw_hi,
  `P-value` = aipw_p
) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(Estimate, SE, `95% CI Lower`, `95% CI Upper`), decimals = 3) |>
  fmt_scientific(columns = `P-value`, decimals = 2) |>
  tab_header(title = "Doubly Robust AIPW Estimator") |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Augmented IPW using outcome models fit separately by treatment arm (OLS on RMST24) and propensity scores from WeightIt. Doubly robust: consistent if either the outcome model or propensity score model is correctly specified."
  ) |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size           = px(13),
    heading.title.font.size   = px(14),
    heading.title.font.weight = "bold",
    table.width               = pct(100)
  )






Table 2b. Doubly Robust AIPW Estimate — RMST Difference at 24 Months.


Doubly Robust AIPW Estimator


Analysis
Estimate
SE
95% CI Lower
95% CI Upper
P-value




AIPW — RMST Difference at 24 Months
2.732

1.122

8.80 × 10−4



Augmented IPW using outcome models fit separately by treatment arm (OLS on RMST24) and propensity scores from WeightIt. Doubly robust: consistent if either the outcome model or propensity score model is correctly specified.

title: Time-to-Event Endpoints — Simulated Oncology RWE
section: 5 Causal Forest for Heterogeneous Treatment Effects
text:
5 Causal Forest for Heterogeneous Treatment Effects


Show code
# RMST at month 24 as continuous outcome
data_onc$rmst24 <- pmin(data_onc$os_time, 24)


# Covariate Matrix
X <- model.matrix(
  ~ age + stage + ecog,
  data = data_onc
)[,-1]


# Fit Causal Forest
W <- data_onc$trt
Y <- data_onc$rmst24

set.seed(42)

cf <- causal_forest(
  X,
  Y,
  W,
  num.trees = 2000,
  honesty = TRUE
)



# Individual Treatment Effects (CATEs)
tau_hat <- predict(cf)$predictions
data_onc$tau_hat <- tau_hat


Interpretation: - Positive τ̂ → treatment increases RMST - Negative τ̂ → treatment decreases RMST

title: Time-to-Event Endpoints — Simulated Oncology RWE
text:
6 Precision Medicine Visualization


Show code
ggplot(
  data_onc,
  aes(
    x = age,
    y = tau_hat,
    color = factor(stage)
  )
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    x = "Age",
    y = "Estimated Individual Treatment Effect (Months RMST)",
    color = "Stage"
  ) +
  theme_bw()





Figure 4. Conditional Average Treatment Effects (CATEs) vs. Age by Stage.

title: Time-to-Event Endpoints — Simulated Oncology RWE
section: 7 Average Treatment Effect (RMST Difference at 24 Months)
text:
7 Average Treatment Effect (RMST Difference at 24 Months)


Show code
# Estimates E[Y(1) - Y(0)]
ate_cf <- average_treatment_effect(cf, target.sample="all")
est <- ate_cf[1]
se  <- ate_cf[2]

z <- est / se
p <- 2 * pnorm(-abs(z))

tibble(
  Analysis = "RMST Difference at 24 Months",
  Estimate = est,
  SE = se,
  `95% CI Lower` = est - 1.96 * se,
  `95% CI Upper` = est + 1.96 * se,
  `P-value` = p
) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(Estimate, SE, `95% CI Lower`, `95% CI Upper`),
    decimals = 3
  ) |>
  fmt_scientific(
    columns = `P-value`,
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Average Treatment Effect (Causal Forest)"
  )






Table 4. RMST Difference at 24 Months.


Average Treatment Effect (Causal Forest)


Analysis
Estimate
SE
95% CI Lower
95% CI Upper
P-value




RMST Difference at 24 Months
2.902
0.862
1.212
4.592
7.65 × 10−4







The causal forest ATE of 2.902 months RMST difference (SE: 0.862; 95% CI: 1.21, 4.59) is directionally consistent with both the IPW-adjusted Cox HR = 0.95 and the AIPW RMST estimate above, providing cross-method validation of the treatment signal. Convergence across estimators — parametric Cox PH, semiparametric IPW, doubly robust AIPW, and nonparametric causal forest — strengthens confidence in the direction and magnitude of the treatment effect.

title: Time-to-Event Endpoints — Simulated Oncology RWE
text:
8 Causal Forest Calibration Test


Show code
cal <- test_calibration(cf)

# Extract directly from the matrix by position — avoids as.data.frame() column
# name instability across grf versions
cal_tbl <- tibble(
  Term     = rownames(cal),
  Estimate = cal[, 1],
  SE       = cal[, 2]
) |>
  mutate(
    CI_Lower = Estimate - 1.96 * SE,
    CI_Upper = Estimate + 1.96 * SE,
    `P-value` = 2 * pnorm(-abs(Estimate / SE))
  )

cal_tbl |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(Estimate, SE, CI_Lower, CI_Upper),
    decimals = 3
  ) |>
  fmt_scientific(
    columns = `P-value`,
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Term = "Calibration Component",
    Estimate = "Estimate",
    SE = "SE",
    CI_Lower = "95% CI Lower",
    CI_Upper = "95% CI Upper"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Causal Forest Calibration Test"
  )






Table 5. Causal Forest Calibration Test.


Causal Forest Calibration Test


Calibration Component
Estimate
SE
95% CI Lower
95% CI Upper
P-value




mean.forest.prediction
1.014
0.283
0.459
1.569
3.41 × 10−4


differential.forest.prediction
0.177
0.594
−0.987
1.342
7.65 × 10−1







The calibration test assesses whether the causal forest’s predicted CATEs (τ̂) are meaningful signals rather than noise. Two components are reported. The mean forest prediction tests whether the average predicted CATE differs from zero — a significant estimate (p < 0.05) confirms that the forest detects a non-zero average treatment effect, consistent with the IPW and AIPW estimates above. The differential forest prediction is the more diagnostic quantity: it tests whether variation in τ̂ across individuals predicts variation in actual outcomes after accounting for the mean. An estimate near 1.0 with a significant p-value indicates well-calibrated heterogeneity — the forest’s rankings of who benefits most are trustworthy. An estimate near 0 suggests the predicted heterogeneity is not supported by the data and individual CATEs should be interpreted with caution.

title: Time-to-Event Endpoints — Simulated Oncology RWE
text:
9 Causal Forest Variable Importance


Show code
vi_tbl <-
  tibble(
    Variable = colnames(X),
    Importance = variable_importance(cf)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(Importance)) |>
  mutate(
    Rank = row_number(),
    Relative_Importance =
      Importance / max(Importance)
  )

vi_tbl |>
  select(
    Rank,
    Variable,
    Importance,
    Relative_Importance
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(Importance, Relative_Importance),
    decimals = 3
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Rank = "Rank",
    Variable = "Covariate",
    Importance = "Variable Importance",
    Relative_Importance = "Relative Importance"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Causal Forest Variable Importance"
  )






Table 6. Causal Forest Variable Importance.


Causal Forest Variable Importance


Rank
Covariate
Variable Importance
Relative Importance




1
age
0.548
1.000


2
ecog
0.241
0.439


3
stage
0.211
0.385







Variable importance in a causal forest measures how frequently each covariate is used as a splitting variable across trees, weighted by the depth at which splits occur — earlier splits receive higher weight because they partition the largest portions of the data. The top-ranked covariate is the primary driver of treatment effect heterogeneity in this simulation: patients differ most in their RMST benefit along that dimension. Relative importance rescales each score to the maximum, making cross-covariate comparisons straightforward — a value of 1.0 is the most influential, values near 0 contribute minimally to heterogeneity. In a precision medicine context, the highest-importance covariate is the natural candidate for subgroup stratification: patients in the upper versus lower quantiles of that variable represent the population segment with the most divergent treatment responses. These importance scores should be interpreted as measures of predictive splitting activity, not as causal effect estimates for the covariates themselves.

title: Longitudinal Mixed-Effects Modeling and Sensitivity Analyses in Simulated Parkinson’s Disease Data
text: A longitudinal analysis for Parkinson’s patient UPDRS scores using MAR multiple imputation (MICE) sensitivity analysis and propensity score matching: a non-randomized, observational, simulated data study

title: Longitudinal Mixed-Effects Modeling and Sensitivity Analyses in Simulated Parkinson’s Disease Data
text: A longitudinal analysis for Parkinson’s patient UPDRS scores using MAR multiple imputation (MICE) sensitivity analysis and propensity score matching: a non-randomized, observational, simulated data study

title: Longitudinal Mixed-Effects Modeling and Sensitivity Analyses in Simulated Parkinson’s Disease Data
text:
2 Executive Summary
This report demonstrates a reproducible longitudinal clinical reporting workflow using R and Quarto in a simulated Parkinson’s disease observational study. Analyses include:

longitudinal mixed-effects modeling,
MAR multiple imputation sensitivity analysis (MICE),
propensity score matching,
treatment effect estimation,
and automated HTML reporting.

This workflow is intended as a methodological demonstration and does not represent a regulatory submission analysis.






NoteKey Features Demonstrated




Longitudinal mixed-effects modeling
Sensitivity analysis workflows
Missing data handling
Propensity score matching
Automated statistical reporting
Interactive HTML reporting with Quarto
Reproducible research workflows
Clinical-style statistical interpretation

title: Longitudinal Mixed-Effects Modeling and Sensitivity Analyses in Simulated Parkinson’s Disease Data
text:
3 Background
We assessed the efficacy of treatment in patients with Parkinson’s disease (PD) using UPDRS (Unified Parkinson’s Disease Rating Scale) scores. In PD research, the UPDRS is the gold standard for measuring motor and non-motor symptoms.

title: Longitudinal Mixed-Effects Modeling and Sensitivity Analyses in Simulated Parkinson’s Disease Data
text:
4 Methods
This was a non-randomized, observational, 2-arm, simulated data study for adults with ages sampled from a normal distribution with mean 70 and standard deviation 12 years. Sex was sampled from a Bernoulli distribution with probability of being female 0.6. Treatment groups were assigned based on age and sex (non-random, simulating selection bias). We simulated longitudinal data for Parkinson’s patients with UPDRS scores measured at baseline, 1 year, and 2 years. The two groups were control and treatment. Longitudinal analysis was used to track changes in UPDRS scores across multiple time points to determine if treatment slows or accelerates disease progression and identifies symptom trajectories (e.g., motor decline, non-motor worsening) and supports trial design and stratification. The study data was simulated so that older and more females tended to get treatment. The primary endpoint was the differences between treatment groups (treatment - control) in UPDRS scores at year 2. No safety analysis was conducted. The ITT analysis set included all patients in the simulated data set. Multiple imputation by chained equations (MICE) under a missing-at-random (MAR) assumption, with M = 25 completed datasets pooled via Rubin’s Rules, and propensity score matching to balance age and sex were utilized for sensitivity analyses.

title: Longitudinal Mixed-Effects Modeling and Sensitivity Analyses in Simulated Parkinson’s Disease Data
text:
5 Analysis
We simulate longitudinal data for Parkinson’s patients with UPDRS scores measured at baseline, 1 year, and 2 years. Two groups: Control and Treatment.


Show code
set.seed(456)
# Non-randomized observational study: older and more females tend to get treatment
n_patients <- 400
patients <- 1:n_patients
times <- c(0, 1, 2)  # baseline, 1 year, 2 years

# Non-random group assignment: function for treatment probability
prob_treatment <- function(age, sex) {
  plogis(-1 + 0.05*(age - 65) + 0.6*(sex == "F"))
}

# Create baseline covariates first
baseline_covariates <- data.frame(
  Patient = as.factor(patients),
  Age = rnorm(n_patients, mean = 70, sd = 12),  # Older population
  Sex = sample(c("M", "F"), n_patients, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.4, 0.6))  # More females
)

# Assign groups based on age and sex (non-random, simulating selection bias)
baseline_covariates$Group <- as.factor(sapply(1:n_patients, function(i) {
  if (runif(1) < prob_treatment(baseline_covariates$Age[i], baseline_covariates$Sex[i])) {
    "Treatment"
  } else {
    "Control"
  }
}))

# Create longitudinal data frame
data <- expand.grid(Patient = patients, Time = times)
data$Patient <- as.factor(data$Patient)
data$Time <- as.factor(data$Time)

# Merge with baseline covariates
data <- data %>% left_join(baseline_covariates, by = "Patient")

# Simulate UPDRS scores
data$UPDRS <- rnorm(nrow(data), mean = 35 + 5 * as.numeric(as.character(data$Time)) + 
                      ifelse(data$Group == "Treatment", -1.5, 0), sd = 6)

# Parameterization of missing data
data$UPDRS <- ifelse(
  runif(nrow(data)) < params$missing_rate &
  data$Time != "0",
  NA,
  data$UPDRS
)

data |>
  head() |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Simulated Data — First Six Rows") |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = px(12))

# Add at the bottom of the simulate-data chunk
data_wide <- data |>
  select(Patient, Time, Age, Sex, Group, UPDRS) |>
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols      = c(Patient, Age, Sex, Group),
    names_from   = Time,
    values_from  = UPDRS,
    names_prefix = "UPDRS_t"
  )



5.1 Computational Reproducibility
Random seeds were pre-specified for all simulation and matching procedures to ensure reproducible analytical outputs.


5.2 Demographics

5.3 Primary Analysis
Fit a linear mixed effects model to estimate least squares means differences between treatment groups at year 2.


Show code
data$Group <- factor(data$Group,
                     levels = c("Control", "Treatment"))
m1 <- lmer(UPDRS ~ Group*Time + Age + (1|Patient), data=data)

source("R/utils.R")


u4 <- extract_emm_table(m1)
u4 |>
  gt() |>
  format_gt_table() |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Table 2. Summary of Linear Mixed-Effects Model Predicting UPDRS by Treatment and Age at Year 2"
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "CI: 95% Confidence Interval; Formula: UPDRS ~ Group * Time + Age + (1 | Patient)"
  )

Show code
# emmeans estimates
est_yr2 <- u4 |> filter(grepl("Treatment - Control", Group)) |> pull(Estimate)
ci_yr2  <- u4 |> filter(grepl("Treatment - Control", Group)) |> pull(CI)


The longitudinal mixed-effects model demonstrated lower estimated UPDRS scores in the treatment group at Year 2 relative to control, consistent with simulated treatment benefit.


Show code
# Summary statistics by treatment group and visit
plot_data <- data %>%
  group_by(Group, Time) %>%
  summarize(
    Mean = mean(UPDRS, na.rm = TRUE),
    SD = sd(UPDRS, na.rm = TRUE),
    N = sum(!is.na(UPDRS)),
    SE = SD / sqrt(N),
    Lower = Mean - 1.96 * SE,
    Upper = Mean + 1.96 * SE,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Convert Time to numeric for plotting
plot_data$Time <- as.numeric(as.character(plot_data$Time))

# Longitudinal profile plot
ggplot(plot_data,
       aes(x = Time,
           y = Mean,
           color = Group,
           group = Group)) +

  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +

  geom_point(size = 3) +

  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = Lower, ymax = Upper),
    width = 0.08,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +

  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(0, 1, 2),
    labels = c("Baseline", "Year 1", "Year 2")
  ) +

  labs(
    title = "Longitudinal Mean UPDRS Profiles by Treatment Group",
    subtitle = "Observed Mean Scores with 95% Confidence Intervals",
    x = "Visit",
    y = "Mean UPDRS Score",
    color = "Treatment Group"
  ) +

  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +

  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "top"
  )





Figure 1. Longitudinal mean UPDRS profiles by treatment group.






Show code
plot(
  fitted(m1),
  resid(m1),
  xlab = "Fitted Values",
  ylab = "Residuals",
  main = "Residuals vs Fitted"
)

abline(h = 0, lty = 2)





Figure 2. Residual diagnostics for the primary mixed-effects model.

title: Longitudinal Mixed-Effects Modeling and Sensitivity Analyses in Simulated Parkinson’s Disease Data
text:
6 Findings
Four hundred patients were assigned to receive control (n=234), or Treatment (n=166). Treatment reduced UPDRS score versus control (treatment 43.35 vs control 46.27; difference -3.01, 95% CI (-4.366, -1.648)).

title: Longitudinal Mixed-Effects Modeling and Sensitivity Analyses in Simulated Parkinson’s Disease Data
text:
7 Interpretation
In patients with Parkinson’s disease, treatment reduced the UPDRS score at Year 2. These findings are based on simulated data and are intended as a methodological demonstration only.

7.1 Missing Data Analysis
Under a Missing At Random (MAR) assumption, missing UPDRS outcomes are predicted by observed covariates (Age, Sex, Group) and observed UPDRS values at other timepoints. Multiple imputation by chained equations (MICE) with predictive mean matching was used to generate M = 25 completed datasets. Fixed-effect estimates from each dataset were combined using Rubin’s Rules to produce a single pooled estimate and standard error that properly accounts for within- and between-imputation uncertainty.

7.1.1 Table 3a. Missingness Summary Prior to Imputation








Table 3a. Missingness Summary Prior to Imputation


UPDRS_t0 (baseline) is fully observed by design


N patients
Missing — Year 1 (n)
Missing — Year 2 (n)
% Missing — Year 1
% Missing — Year 2




400
114
86
28.5%
21.5%









7.1.2 Figure 3a





Figure 3a. Missing data pattern across patients and visits.






Show code
# ── 1. Prepare data for mice ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Remove Patient ID before passing to mice (high-cardinality ID should not
# be a predictor). Store IDs separately and re-attach after completion.

patient_ids <- data_wide |> pull(Patient)

data_mice <- data_wide |>
  select(-Patient) |>
  mutate(
    Sex   = factor(Sex,   levels = c("M", "F")),
    Group = factor(Group, levels = c("Control", "Treatment"))
  )

# ── 2. Configure predictor matrix and imputation methods ──────────────────────
ini  <- mice(data_mice, maxit = 0, print = FALSE)
meth <- ini$method
pred <- ini$predictorMatrix

# Baseline UPDRS is fully observed — exclude from imputation target
# but retain as a predictor for Year 1 and Year 2 imputation
meth["UPDRS_t0"] <- ""
pred["UPDRS_t0", ] <- 0

# pmm (predictive mean matching) is appropriate for bounded continuous scores:
# it draws observed values close to the linear prediction, preserving the
# empirical distribution and avoiding impossible out-of-range imputations
meth[c("UPDRS_t1", "UPDRS_t2")] <- "pmm"

# ── 3. Run mice ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
set.seed(2026)
imp_mar <- mice(
  data_mice,
  m               = 25,    # 25 datasets: adequate for ~25% missingness
  method          = meth,
  predictorMatrix = pred,
  maxit           = 15,    # iterations per dataset until convergence
  print           = FALSE
)




Show code
# Trace plots: flat, well-mixed chains indicate convergence
plot(imp_mar, c("UPDRS_t1", "UPDRS_t2"), layout = c(2, 2))





Figure 3b. Convergence diagnostics for mice imputation. Mean and SD of imputed values should mix well (no trend) across iterations.






Show code
# ── 4. Fit LMER on each completed dataset ─────────────────────────────────────
# For each of the M imputed wide datasets:
#   (a) re-attach Patient IDs
#   (b) reshape wide → long
#   (c) fit the same LMER specification as the primary analysis

fit_on_completed <- function(i) {
  
  wide_i <- mice::complete(imp_mar, action = i) |>
    mutate(Patient = patient_ids)                  # restore patient ID
  
  long_i <- wide_i |>
    pivot_longer(
      cols         = starts_with("UPDRS_t"),
      names_to     = "Time",
      names_prefix = "UPDRS_t",
      values_to    = "UPDRS"
    ) |>
    mutate(
      Time  = factor(Time,  levels = c("0", "1", "2")),
      Group = factor(Group, levels = c("Control", "Treatment"))
    )
  
  lmer(UPDRS ~ Group * Time + Age + (1 | Patient), data = long_i)
}

models_mar <- lapply(seq_len(imp_mar$m), fit_on_completed)

# ── 5. Pool fixed effects via Rubin's Rules ───────────────────────────────────
# mice::pool() uses broom.mixed::tidy() to extract estimates from each lmerMod
pooled_mar  <- mice::as.mira(models_mar) |> mice::pool()
pooled_summ <- summary(pooled_mar, conf.int = TRUE)

# ── 6. Display pooled fixed effects table ─────────────────────────────────────
pooled_summ |>
  as_tibble() |>
  rename(
    Parameter = term,
    Estimate  = estimate,
    SE        = std.error,
    df        = df,
    p         = p.value,
    Lower     = `2.5 %`,
    Upper     = `97.5 %`
  ) |>
  mutate(
    across(c(Estimate, SE, Lower, Upper), \(x) round(x, 3)),
    CI = paste0("(", round(Lower, 2), ", ", round(Upper, 2), ")"),
    p  = case_when(
           p < 0.001 ~ "< 0.001",
           TRUE      ~ as.character(round(p, 3))
         )
  ) |>
  select(Parameter, Estimate, SE, CI, df, p) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Table 3b. Pooled Fixed Effects — MAR Sensitivity Analysis",
    subtitle = "MICE with Predictive Mean Matching; m = 25; Rubin's Rules"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Parameter = "Parameter",
    Estimate  = "Estimate",
    SE        = "Std. Error",
    CI        = "95% CI",
    df        = "Pooled df",
    p         = "p-value"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Pooled using Rubin's Rules. Formula: UPDRS ~ Group × Time + Age + (1 | Patient). FMI reported in Table 3c."
  ) |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size           = px(13),
    heading.title.font.size   = px(14),
    heading.title.font.weight = "bold",
    table.width               = pct(100)
  )








Table 3b. Pooled Fixed Effects — MAR Sensitivity Analysis

Pooled using Rubin's Rules. Formula: UPDRS ~ Group × Time + Age + (1 | Patient). FMI reported in Table 3c.










Show code
# ── 7. Pooled treatment effect at Year 2 formatted like primary analysis ──────
# emmeans is computed for each imputed model, then estimates and SEs are
# combined using Rubin's Rules. The Satterthwaite df from emmeans is used
# in the Barnard-Rubin (1999) degrees-of-freedom approximation.

pool_emmeans_contrast <- function(models, time_level = "2") {
  
  # Extract Treatment - Control contrast at the requested timepoint
  extract_one <- function(mod) {
    em  <- emmeans(mod, ~ Group | Time)
    ct  <- contrast(em, method = "trt.vs.ctrl", ref = "Control")
    row <- as.data.frame(ct) |> filter(Time == time_level)
    list(
      est = row$estimate,
      se  = row$SE,
      df  = row$df      # Satterthwaite df
    )
  }
  
  res   <- lapply(models, extract_one)
  M     <- length(res)
  ests  <- sapply(res, `[[`, "est")
  ses   <- sapply(res, `[[`, "se")
  dfs   <- sapply(res, `[[`, "df")
  
  # Rubin's Rules
  Q_bar <- mean(ests)                          # pooled estimate
  U_bar <- mean(ses^2)                         # average within-imputation variance
  B     <- var(ests)                           # between-imputation variance
  T_var <- U_bar + (1 + 1 / M) * B            # total variance
  SE_p  <- sqrt(T_var)
  
  # Fraction of missing information (FMI)
  r   <- (1 + 1 / M) * B / U_bar
  fmi <- r / (1 + r)
  
  # Barnard-Rubin (1999) pooled degrees of freedom
  df_old <- mean(dfs)
  df_m   <- (M - 1) / r^2
  df_obs <- (df_old + 1) / (df_old + 3) * df_old * (1 - r)
  df_p   <- 1 / (1 / df_m + 1 / df_obs)
  
  ci <- Q_bar + qt(c(0.025, 0.975), df = df_p) * SE_p
  
  tibble(
    Contrast    = "Treatment \u2212 Control",
    Time        = paste0("Year ", time_level),
    Estimate    = round(Q_bar, 3),
    SE          = round(SE_p,  3),
    CI          = paste0("(", round(ci[1], 2), ", ", round(ci[2], 2), ")"),
    FMI         = round(fmi, 3)
  )
}

contrast_pooled <- pool_emmeans_contrast(models_mar, time_level = "2")

contrast_pooled |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Table 3c. Pooled Treatment Effect at Year 2 — MAR Sensitivity Analysis",
    subtitle = "MICE / Predictive Mean Matching; m = 25; Rubin's Rules"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Contrast = "Contrast",
    Time     = "Timepoint",
    Estimate = "Estimate",
    SE       = "Std. Error",
    CI       = "95% CI",
    FMI      = "FMI"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = paste0(
      "FMI: Fraction of Missing Information \u2014 proportion of total variance ",
      "attributable to missing data. Higher values indicate stronger sensitivity ",
      "to the MAR assumption. df computed via Barnard-Rubin (1999) approximation."
    )
  ) |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size           = px(13),
    heading.title.font.size   = px(14),
    heading.title.font.weight = "bold",
    table.width               = pct(100)
  )








Table 3c. Pooled Treatment Effect at Year 2 — MAR Sensitivity Analysis


MICE / Predictive Mean Matching; m = 25; Rubin's Rules

FMI: Fraction of Missing Information — proportion of total variance attributable to missing data. Higher values indicate stronger sensitivity to the MAR assumption. df computed via Barnard-Rubin (1999) approximation.








Results from the MAR multiple imputation sensitivity analysis are presented in Tables 3b and 3c. Consistency with the primary analysis estimates would support robustness of the primary findings to the missing-at-random assumption. The Fraction of Missing Information (FMI) quantifies the proportion of uncertainty attributable to missing data; values below 0.10 are generally considered low.



7.2 Propensity Score Matching
Assuming the groups are not randomized, use propensity score matching to balance covariates of age and sex. Based on the matched data, fit the longitudinal mixed-effects model and estimate least squares means differences between treatment groups at year 2.


Show code
set.seed(2026)

# Propensity score matching and evaluation

# Subset orginal data based on patients identified by Propensity score matching
matched_pid <- matched_data2 |> select(Patient)
matched_data_final <- left_join(matched_pid, data, by = "Patient")


# Generate linear mixed effects model on matched population
matched_data_final$Group <- factor(
    matched_data_final$Group, 
    levels = c("Control", "Treatment"))

title: Longitudinal Mixed-Effects Modeling and Sensitivity Analyses in Simulated Parkinson’s Disease Data
text:
8 Conclusions
This report demonstrates a reproducible and automated longitudinal clinical reporting workflow using modern R and Quarto methodologies. The analysis integrates mixed-effects modeling, missing data sensitivity analyses, and propensity score matching within a publication-style HTML reporting framework suitable for exploratory clinical and real-world evidence applications.
The workflow illustrates approaches commonly used in pharmaceutical and biotechnology clinical development environments for statistical analysis, reproducibility, and reporting automation.

title: Longitudinal Mixed-Effects Modeling and Sensitivity Analyses in Simulated Parkinson’s Disease Data
text:
9 Limitations
This report uses simulated observational data for methodological demonstration purposes. Results should not be interpreted clinically. Missing data sensitivity analyses use MICE with predictive mean matching under a missing-at-random (MAR) assumption. Whether the MAR assumption holds cannot be verified from the observed data alone; MNAR sensitivity analyses (e.g., reference-based methods using the rbmi package) would be required for regulatory-grade submissions.

title: Longitudinal Mixed-Effects Modeling and Sensitivity Analyses in Simulated Parkinson’s Disease Data
text:
10 Reproducibility
This report was generated using a reproducible Quarto workflow in R.
All analyses, figures, tables, and statistical outputs were generated programmatically from simulated source data.
Key components include:

automated data simulation,
longitudinal mixed-effects modeling,
MAR multiple imputation using MICE (m = 25, Rubin’s Rules),
propensity score matching,
reproducible statistical reporting,
and parameterized report generation.

The workflow is designed to reflect modern clinical reporting and real-world evidence analysis practices used in pharmaceutical and biotechnology settings.

title: Longitudinal Mixed-Effects Modeling and Sensitivity Analyses in Simulated Parkinson’s Disease Data
text:
